nativeRange.habitat: Tropical wetlands, marshes, and shallow stream margins and creek beds in Sri Lanka, with reported occurrence in adjacent south-western India. In the wild, the plant is typically rooted in wet soil or shallow water along creek banks and is only fully submerged during seasonal flooding; for most of the year it grows emersed or semi-emersed in moist conditions.

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate or fine gravel with root tabs
rhizome anchored on or just above the substrate (do not bury the rhizome)
moderate to bright lighting to develop leaf coloration
ample vertical and footprint space for full-sized specimens (up to ~90 cm tall)
stable parameters to reduce acclimation melt

avoidWith: Silver Dollars and other plant-eating fish

summary: Lagenandra ovata (Malayan Sword) is a large, broad-leaved aroid from Sri Lanka that can reach 30 to 90 cm tall. It works best as a background or centerpiece plant in larger tanks, and like its relative Cryptocoryne, it tends to shed leaves during acclimation before regrowing them.
careNotes: Lagenandra ovata, commonly sold as the Malayan Sword or Sri Lanka Lagenandra, is a large rosette aroid closely related to Cryptocoryne — both genera belong to the family Araceae. Native to Sri Lanka, with reports of occurrence in adjacent south-western India, this is the type species of the genus Lagenandra. In the wild it grows along shallow stream margins and creek beds, typically rooted in wet soil or shallow water and only fully inundated during seasonal flooding; most of its growing season is spent emersed or semi-emersed. Mature plants are substantial — reported at 30 to 90 cm tall, with broad, lance- to paddle-shaped leaves carried on long petioles (the leaf stems, which can reach 1 to 2 feet in habitat). This makes it a true centerpiece or background species rather than a midground filler, and it is best suited to tanks of at least 100 litres with 60 cm or more of tank length. CO2 supplementation is not required but is reported to support faster, denser growth. Lagenandra ovata tends to be a heavy root feeder, meaning it draws most of its nutrients through the rhizome (the thick horizontal stem at the base of the plant) and roots rather than from the water column. A nutrient-rich substrate or fine gravel supplemented with root tabs is strongly recommended; liquid fertiliser added to the water column is secondary. IMPORTANT PLANTING NOTE: like all Lagenandra, and like the related genera Anubias and Bucephalandra, the rhizome must NOT be buried. Plant so the roots are anchored in the substrate but the rhizome itself sits on or just above the surface; burying the rhizome causes it to rot. MELT WARNING: like Cryptocoryne, Lagenandra ovata commonly sheds existing leaves during acclimation or after significant water-parameter shifts. This is a normal stress response; new leaves adapted to tank conditions generally regrow within 2 to 4 weeks. Do not discard the plant during a melt — the rhizome and roots are the living core. Growers commonly report that this species thrives best in shallow-water or emersed conditions (such as a paludarium — a tank that combines land and water areas), and its reputation for being difficult in the hobby is largely attributed to attempts at full submersion in tanks with unstable parameters. Note that the name Lagenandra thwaitesii is sometimes listed in the trade as a synonym for this species, but L. thwaitesii is a separate Sri Lankan species and the two should not be confused.
breedingNotes: Lagenandra ovata is propagated vegetatively by dividing the creeping rhizome into sections, each retaining leaves and roots, or by separating side-shoot plantlets once they have developed their own root system. Under emersed cultivation the species can flower, producing an inflorescence (a flowering structure) typical of the aroid family: a dark purple-to-black spathe and spadix reaching around 6 to 8 inches in length. Underwater flowering is rare, and seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium.

primary.name: Aqua-Fish.Net
primary.notes: Primary source after swap: the assigned primary (Tropica) and secondary (Buce Plant) catalogs do not carry Lagenandra ovata at access time. Aqua-Fish.Net provided: native range (Sri Lanka, tropical wetlands/marshes/riverbanks), size 30-90 cm, family Araceae, medium light, temperature 19-26 C, pH 6.5-7.2, gH 0-18 dGH, fine-gravel substrate, background placement, medium care difficulty, propagation by runners/plantlets and rhizome division, rhizome must not be fully buried, suitable for submerged or emersed culture.

additional.name: Aquarium Plants Factory (APF)
additional.notes: Cross-checked Sri Lanka origin, rosette growth form, broad ovate leaves with undulating edges, moderate-to-high light preference, CO2 not required but beneficial, medium growth rate, pH 5.5-7.5 (used as the soft-end lower bound), midground/background placement, easy-to-moderate care difficulty, nutrient-rich substrate preference, propagation by runners/rhizome, submerged or emersed cultivation. NOTE: APF cross-references 'Lagenandra thwaitesii' as a synonym; this is botanically incorrect — L. thwaitesii is a separate Sri Lankan species. Trade common name 'Malayan Sword' captured here.

additional.name: Brian's Botanicals — Lagenandra ovata, a neglected gem
additional.notes: Specialty grower commentary: confirmed Sri Lanka + tropical Asia (India) native range on creek beds; size 1-3 ft (~30-90 cm) with 1-2 ft petioles; involute leaf vernation diagnostic of genus Lagenandra; thrives in shallow water / emersed tray culture and is typically only submerged during seasonal flooding; temperature tolerance 65-90 F (18-32 C); benefits from diluted root-zone fertilizer (~10% dose, 1-2 pinches per gallon); rarely flowers underwater; dark purple-black 6-8-inch inflorescence; characterized as 'tender' in the hobby due to preference for shallow water over full submersion.

additional.name: International Aroid Society — Lagenandra ovata (L.) Thwaites
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority page. Confirmed: perennial aquatic plant native to Sri Lanka, glossy leaves, adapts to both submerged and emergent conditions. Family Araceae, order Alismatales confirmed via genus-level reference. Stub-level detail otherwise.

additional.name: Tropica (search index)
additional.notes: Documented assigned-primary failure: Tropica catalog does not list Lagenandra ovata (only L. meeboldii 'Red'). Source swap to Aqua-Fish.Net as primary was performed per playbook step 3 fallback rule.

additional.name: Buce Plant (search index)
additional.notes: Documented assigned-secondary failure: Buce Plant search returned 'No results for lagenandra ovata' at access time. Available Lagenandra species in catalog: meeboldii Red, meeboldii Green, keralensis, thwaitesii (sold out).
